title: Understanding Tenses: How to Help at Home
whatIsIt: Tenses tell us WHEN something happens — past, present, or future. Aspects tell us whether the action is complete, ongoing, or repeated. For example, 'I eat' (present, simple), 'I am eating' (present, ongoing), and 'I have eaten' (present, complete) all describe different ways an action exists in time.
howToHelp: When telling stories together, point out time words: 'yesterday', 'right now', 'tomorrow' — these are like little flags that tell us which tense to use.
Play 'What are you doing?' games — describe actions happening NOW versus what HAPPENED earlier. This builds the instinct for present continuous versus past simple.
Read books together and ask: 'Is this happening now or did it already happen?' Encourage your child to find time words as clues.
When watching movies, pause and ask: 'What will happen next?' This naturally practices future tense in a fun context.
Keep a simple daily diary together — write what happened today using past tense. Even three sentences a day builds powerful habits.
Use a physical timeline: draw a line with past-present-future and place events on it. Visual learners especially benefit from seeing time laid out in space.
commonMistakes: Mixing up 'I go' and 'I went' — this is completely normal at this stage! The irregular past forms take time to memorize.
Forgetting the -s on third person: 'She go' instead of 'She goes' — children often don't hear the final -s in fast speech.
Using 'yesterday' with present tense: 'Yesterday I go to school' — this shows they're learning time words before they fully master tense shifts.
Overusing continuous tense: 'I am liking pizza' instead of 'I like pizza' — state verbs (like, want, know) don't usually take continuous forms.
signsOfProgress: Self-corrects without being told ('I go... I mean, I went') — this shows their inner grammar monitor is waking up!
Uses time words naturally in conversation: 'Yesterday I played', 'Tomorrow I will swim'.
Can tell a story with mostly correct tenses, even if some irregular verbs are still tricky.
Asks questions about unusual verb forms: 'Why is it went, not goed?' — curiosity about exceptions is a hallmark of deep learning.
Notices when characters in books use the wrong tense — this shows they're developing an ear for grammar.
ifStruggling: Focus on ONE tense at a time — don't overwhelm with all of them. Master simple present before moving to continuous.
Use physical timelines: draw past-present-future and place stickers for events. Many children need to see time to understand it.
It's okay to make mistakes! Every error is a step toward understanding. Celebrate attempts, not just correctness.
If they get frustrated, take a break — come back when they're curious. Learning happens best in a state of safety and play.
Celebrate when they notice tenses in everyday life, even if not perfect. Awareness comes before accuracy.
Sing songs with different tenses — music makes grammar sticky and joyful.
